query: methotrexate treatment effectiveness in rheumatoid arthritis

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management -- ACR and EULAR Treatment Guidelines
retrieved_docs.content: If disease activity remains high despite a TNF inhibitor switch to a non-TNF biologic agent with or without methotrexate

retrieved_docs.title: Methotrexate -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Methotrexate is a folic acid antagonist indicated for the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis because of its high potency and efficacy; it can also help treat patients with juvenile idiopathic arthritis. [1] Gubner first suggested methotrexate as a treatment for rheumatoid arthritis after performing a double-blinded, placebo-controlled clinical trial of methotrexate in patients with this condition. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management -- ACR and EULAR Treatment Guidelines
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with low disease activity and early disease, monotherapy with methotrexate is the preferred treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Disease-Modifying Antirheumatic Drugs (DMARD) -- Indications -- DMARDs in Rheumatoid Arthritis
retrieved_docs.content: Although many medications can be used in the treatment of RA, methotrexate is the most commonly used agent as an initial treatment. RA treatment is complicated, with several factors playing a role in decision making, including disease activity and severity, comorbidities, and patient preference (including cost, route of administration, and frequency of monitoring). RA treatment can be either monotherapy or combination therapy, although several randomized controlled trials have shown the superiority of combination therapy of a biologic DMARD with a conventional DMARD such as methotrexate over either agent alone. Treatment goals shall include achieving remission or low disease activity and preventing radiographic progression of the disease. The initiation of therapy early in the disease has been shown to prevent radiographic progression, most of which occurs within the first few months of the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management -- ACR and EULAR Treatment Guidelines
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with established RA, who are DMARD naive, methotrexate is the preferred agent for initial monotherapy, regardless of the disease activity level.

retrieved_docs.title: Hand and Wrist Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological Management
retrieved_docs.content: The use of oral methotrexate is conditionally recommended over the subcutaneous route for patients initiating methotrexate.

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) typically used in treating RA include methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine, sulfasalazine, and leflunomide. Anti-TNF-alpha inhibitors include etanercept, infliximab, adalimumab, golimumab, and certolizumab pegol. Other biologic DMARDs include interleukin (IL) 6 inhibitors such as tocilizumab and sarilumab, T-cell costimulation inhibitors such as abatacept (CTLA4-Ig), and the anti-CD20 B-cell depleting monoclonal antibody such as rituximab. Targeted synthetic DMARDs include Janus kinases (JAK) inhibitors such as tofacitinib, baricitinib, and upadacitinib. [2] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Hand and Wrist Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological Management
retrieved_docs.content: Initiation/titration of methotrexate to at least 15 mg as a weekly dose within 4 to 6 weeks is conditionally recommended over initiation/titration to <15 mg weekly dose.

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management -- Nonbiologic DMARDs
retrieved_docs.content: This category includes methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine (HCQ), azathioprine (AZA), sulfasalazine, leflunomide, and cyclosporine. Methotrexate is the initial drug of choice for patients with RA. The recommended treatment plan recommends an initial dose of 10-15 mg/week of methotrexate with an escalation of 5 mg/month and a target dose of 20-25 mg/week. [98] Subcutaneous administration can be tried in patients who do not respond adequately or are intolerant to oral administration. [99] In patients on methotrexate, 15 mg/week or higher, who still have some disease activity, splitting the dose (10 mg in the morning and 10 mg in the evening every week) increased the bioavailability of methotrexate. [100] Triple therapy, consisting of the combination of methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine, and sulfasalazine, has been shown to be an effective regimen for RA. [101] [102] However, triple therapy is not well tolerated, and a large percentage of patients do stay on this therapy. [103] [104] [105]

retrieved_docs.title: Hand and Wrist Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological Management
retrieved_docs.content: For patients not tolerating oral weekly methotrexate a split oral dose of over 24 hours or weekly subcutaneous dose, and/or increasing the dose of  folic/folinic acid, is conditionally recommended over switching other DMARDs.

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management -- Rituximab
retrieved_docs.content: Rituximab is a biologic DMARD that can be added for treating RA if patients have uncontrolled RA and who did not respond to TNF inhibitors. [108] Rituximab is given as an intravenous infusion; it depletes CD20+ B-cells and decreases the immune response to vaccines in patients receiving rituximab. Responses to rituximab are better if the patients are seropositive and if the patients are also on methotrexate. As stated above, it is a preferred agent in patients with underlying lymphoproliferative disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management -- Abatacept
retrieved_docs.content: Abatacept inhibits T-cell activation by binding to CD80 and CD86. It is administered as a monthly intravenous infusion or as a weekly subcutaneous injection. Patients with uncontrolled RA, who have shown an inadequate response to methotrexate and TNF inhibitor therapy, benefit from abatacept therapy with proven efficacy from 6 months to 5 years of therapy. [109]

retrieved_docs.title: Methotrexate -- Administration -- Adult Dosage
retrieved_docs.content: After the initiation of methotrexate therapy, follow-up testing should include cell count (CBC), renal function, and liver function monitoring, which are recommended weekly for 4 weeks and then at least bi-monthly. [22] Rheumatoid arthritis: The recommended starting dosage is 7.5 mg once weekly, which may be adjusted to achieve an optimal therapeutic response. Polyarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis: The recommended starting dosage is 10 mg/m² once weekly, which may be adjusted to achieve an optimal therapeutic response. Psoriasis: The recommended dosage is between 10 and 25 mg once weekly. Once a satisfactory therapeutic response is achieved, the dose should be reduced to the lowest effective dose.

retrieved_docs.title: Methotrexate -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Methotrexate (MTX) is an anti-metabolite commonly administered during chemotherapy and as an immunosuppressant for autoimmune diseases. This activity reviews the indications, mechanism of action, and contraindications for methotrexate as a treatment for neoplastic and autoimmune conditions. Adverse events, off-label uses, pharmacokinetics, administration methods, and key monitoring considerations are discussed to optimize patient outcomes. Relevant drug interactions and clinical toxicology are also highlighted to enhance safe and effective use.

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management -- ACR and EULAR Treatment Guidelines
retrieved_docs.content: If still uncontrolled despite the above trials, use tofacitinib

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management -- ACR and EULAR Treatment Guidelines
retrieved_docs.content: Leflunomide or sulfasalazine are the first-line treatment in patients with a contraindication to methotrexate or intolerance to it.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1702
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIINFLAMMATORY AND ANTIRHEUMATIC PRODUCTS represent a therapeutic class that includes both specific antirheumatic agents and formulations that combine anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic drugs. By encompassing individual agents targeted at rheumatic diseases as well as combination products that provide synergistic anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic effects, this class offers a broad range of options for managing inflammatory and rheumatic conditions in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1544
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic products constitute a broad category of medications that includes antiinflammatory/antirheumatic agents used in combination.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1366
retrieved_docs.content: Aspirin is frequently noted to co‑occur with digoxin, indicating that these two drugs are often prescribed together or encountered in the same patient populations, which may reflect overlapping therapeutic needs such as cardiovascular management alongside anti‑inflammatory treatment. In addition to its well‑known antiplatelet and analgesic effects, aspirin may also be used to treat rheumatoid arthritis and related inflammatory diseases, providing symptomatic relief through its anti‑inflammatory properties. Recognizing both the common concurrent use of aspirin with digoxin and its potential role in managing rheumatoid arthritis helps clinicians coordinate therapy, monitor for drug interactions, and optimize overall patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1598
retrieved_docs.content: Gold preparations are a class of therapeutic agents that contain gold and are used primarily as disease‑modifying antirheumatic drugs. Auranofin 3 mg oral capsule is an example of a gold preparation, classified as an auranofin formulation administered orally. Aurothiomalate sodium is also a member of the gold preparations class. Both auranofin and aurothiomalate sodium share the anti‑inflammatory properties of gold compounds and are employed in the management of rheumatoid arthritis and related conditions. Recognizing that these agents belong to the same gold preparation category helps clinicians anticipate similar efficacy and safety profiles, including potential adverse effects such as dermatitis, nephrotoxicity, and bone‑marrow suppression, and guides appropriate selection of gold‑based therapy in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1544
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, which are classified under the broader category of داء الكولاجين-الوعائي (collagen vascular disease), can be treated with specific medications. Prednisone is recognized as a therapeutic option for managing systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, and hydroxychloroquine is also noted for its efficacy in this condition. In contrast, the drugs trioxsalen and zlato are contraindicated in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders and should be avoided due to potential adverse effects. Awareness of these treatment options and contraindications is essential for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1817
retrieved_docs.content: Prednisone, rimexolone, and paramethasone are all members of the class of systemic glucocorticoids. Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a group of steroid medications that are administered throughout the body to exert anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. By being part of this class, each of these agents shares the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoids, including modulation of gene expression, reduction of cytokine production, and suppression of immune cell activity, which are leveraged in the treatment of a wide range of inflammatory and autoimmune conditions. Understanding that prednisone, rimexolone, and paramethasone belong to the systemic glucocorticoid category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and apply consistent monitoring strategies across these related drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Quinolines, antirheumatic drugs are classified as specific antirheumatic agents, indicating that this subgroup of quinoline compounds is used specifically to treat rheumatic conditions within the broader category of antirheumatic medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2674
retrieved_docs.content: Alergeny is described as having the structural class of auranofin, indicating that the chemical framework of alergeny is related to the gold‑containing compound auranofin. Recognizing this structural relationship helps clarify how alergeny may share pharmacologic properties, mechanisms of action, or potential cross‑reactivity with auranofin, which is used in the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis. Understanding such structural classifications is important for anticipating therapeutic effects, side‑effect profiles, and guiding patient‑specific medication decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2203
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids represent a class of corticosteroid medications that includes agents such as prednison and paramethason. Both prednison and paramethason are synthetic glucocorticoid drugs administered systemically to exert potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. By belonging to the systemic glucocorticoid category, these agents share common mechanisms of action, pharmacologic properties, and therapeutic indications, making them useful in the management of a wide range of inflammatory, autoimmune, and allergic conditions. Understanding that prednison and paramethason are members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, anticipate class‑related side effects, and provide comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 1293
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders is classified as a subtype of the broader collagen‑vascular disease category (داء الكولاجين-الوعائي). Several immunomodulatory and anti‑inflammatory agents are considered therapeutic options for this condition. Prednisone, triamcinolonacetonid (triamcinolone acetonide), and hydroxychlorochin (hydroxychloroquine) may be used to treat systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, reflecting their roles in suppressing autoimmune activity and reducing disease‑related inflammation. Conversely, trioxsalen and zlato are contraindicated in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, indicating that these agents should be avoided because of potential adverse effects or disease exacerbation. Understanding both the beneficial treatments and the contraindicated substances is essential for safe and effective management of patients with this collagen‑vascular disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a class of corticosteroid drugs that exert broad anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive actions throughout the body. Both prednison and rimexolone are individual agents that belong to this systemic glucocorticoid class, sharing the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoid receptor activation, suppression of cytokine production, and modulation of immune cell function. Recognizing prednison and rimexolone as members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians understand their therapeutic indications, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, which are characteristic of systemic glucocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1174
retrieved_docs.content: shared therapeutic goals for musculoskeletal and connective‑tissue conditions. This integrated framework supports comprehensive patient care by aligning specific modalities with appropriate clinical indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 397
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIINFLAMMATORY AND ANTIRHEUMATIC PRODUCTS are classified within the broader therapeutic group of MUSCULO‑SKELETAL SYSTEM DRUGS, indicating that they are used to treat conditions affecting the musculoskeletal system such as inflammation, pain, and rheumatic disorders. Within this category, there are specific formulations referred to as antiinflammatory/antirheumatic agents in combination, which represent combination drug products that combine two or more anti‑inflammatory or antirheumatic agents to enhance therapeutic efficacy. Together, these relationships highlight that anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic therapies are integral components of musculoskeletal pharmacotherapy, and that combination agents constitute a specialized subset designed to provide synergistic treatment options for patients with inflammatory and rheumatic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1665
retrieved_docs.content: Auranofin 3 mg oral capsule is classified as a gold preparation, representing a member of the gold‑based drug class that is used therapeutically, particularly in the management of conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1204
retrieved_docs.content: Deflazacort, prednison, rimexolone, and paramethason are all members of the drug class identified as Glucocorticoids, Systemic. As systemic glucocorticoids, these agents share anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are used in the management of a wide range of inflammatory, autoimmune, and allergic conditions. Recognizing that each of these medications belongs to the same systemic glucocorticoid category helps clinicians understand their similar mechanisms of action, potential side‑effect profiles, and therapeutic indications, facilitating appropriate selection and monitoring in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1460
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders are classified as a subset of the broader disease category داء الكولاجين-الوعائي (vascular collagen disease). Therapeutically, triamcinolonacetonid, dexamethasonisonikotinát, and hydroxychlorochin (hydroxychloroquine) may be employed to treat systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, offering anti‑inflammatory and immunomodulatory benefits. In contrast, trioxsalen and zlato (gold) are contraindicated in patients with these disorders, indicating that their use should be avoided due to potential adverse effects. Recognizing the hierarchical relationship of these conditions and the specific agents that are beneficial versus harmful supports informed clinical decision‑making and safer patient care.
